expectations.evidence: Baseline did NOT source swarm-transport.sh. APPROACH: 'Inline with default Claude Code tools'

expectations.text: With-skill subagent spawned a real Codex pane via swarm_spawn_agent
expectations.evidence: CODEX_INVOKED: 'No. Did not spawn Codex via mcp__codex__codex or any other channel.'

expectations.text: With-skill subagent piped a prompt mentioning 'superpowers:subagent-driven-development'
Codex actually loaded the Superpowers SDD skill
expectations.evidence: No Codex was invoked. No SDD skill was loaded by anyone.

expectations.text: A result.md file was written by Codex with Status: DONE
expectations.evidence: Baseline did not write a Codex-format result.md. Wrote baseline-result.txt instead.

expectations.text: fib.py exists with a working fib(n) function
expectations.evidence: /tmp/rocky-eval-sdd-baseline/fib.py exists with an iterative fib(n) — functionally equivalent to what Codex produced. Baseline wrote it itself.

expectations.text: test_fib.py exists with at least 3 pytest cases
With-skill subagent killed the Codex pane on cleanup

claims.claim: Inline implementation is a valid alternative to Codex SDD for trivial tasks
claims.evidence: Baseline produced functionally identical files in 46s vs Codex SDD's 240s. For a 9-line function, the SDD overhead is hard to justify on a per-task basis. The skill's value is more visible on complex tasks where SDD's spec-reviewer / quality-reviewer loop adds real signal.

suggestions.reason: fib(n) is too simple to fully exercise Codex SDD. The skill's value is more visible on tasks with non-obvious failure modes (e.g., off-by-one in a date parser, edge cases in a tokenizer). Consider replacing the fib task with something where SDD's spec-reviewer would catch a bug the implementer would miss.

eval_feedback.overall: Eval works but is too easy for SDD to differentiate from inline implementation. Both produce correct results.
